title: Rural life: I will no longer be ashamed of being materialistic
gist: A personal essay arguing that treasured possessions, not just people and places, constitute part of one's sense of 'home', reframing materialism as a legitimate emotional anchor rather than something to be ashamed of.

themes.theme: Redefining home beyond place and people

insights.claim: The author, who moved from London to rural Wales, came to realize she has 'many homes' after noticing that a longing for her own possessions (artwork, furniture), not just place, drove her feeling of wanting to be home after travel.
insights.speaker: Kiran Sidhu
insights.so_what: Illustrates a broader cultural shift toward treating personal possessions as identity-anchors rather than mere consumption — relevant to how brands and products are marketed around 'belonging' and self-expression.
insights.locator: All that travel led me to realise something

insights.claim: The author argues that prevailing social norms pressure people to minimize the emotional importance of possessions ('what's a guitar or a picture compared with our friendships and family relationships?'), and that this norm is worth resisting.
insights.speaker: Kiran Sidhu
insights.so_what: A window into a recurring cultural tension between minimalism/anti-materialism messaging and genuine attachment to objects — relevant to consumer psychology narratives.
insights.quote: In order not to be considered materialistic, we are not supposed to think that our possessions are worth much in the grand scheme of things
insights.locator: In order not to be considered materialistic

insights.claim: The author's friend Damian, a musician, described his sense of home as being 'among his eight guitars', which the author uses as the anecdote that reframed her own thinking about possessions and identity.
insights.speaker: Kiran Sidhu
insights.so_what: A specific, human illustration of how professional/creative tools can become identity-defining possessions, not just utilitarian assets.
insights.locator: He said he was looking forward to being among his eight guitars

insights.claim: The author treated sharing a curated box of personal objects (a painting, an embossed mirror, a candlestick, and specific books including Sartre's Existentialism and Humanism) with a new romantic partner as a relationship-defining test of compatibility before he had met her friends or family.
insights.speaker: Kiran Sidhu
insights.so_what: A concrete example of possessions being used as a proxy for emotional intimacy and vetting compatibility, ahead of traditional social introductions.
insights.locator: When I first started dating the man who would become my husband

insights.claim: The author concludes that inanimate objects are not merely decorative but can function as psychological anchors that 'lead us back to ourselves' when things feel senseless, framing her own house as 'a museum dedicated to me.'
insights.speaker: Kiran Sidhu
insights.so_what: A framework for understanding consumer attachment to objects as identity-maintenance rather than pure acquisitiveness.
insights.quote: The house that I live in is a museum dedicated to me.
insights.locator: Inanimate objects aren't always just decorative
